description:
The Department of Food Production (DFP), Faculty of Food and Agriculture, has adopted a comprehensive, systems-based approach to sustainable waste management through interdisciplinary research and applied projects that transform agricultural and food waste into value-added products for food production and environmental remediation. This paper presents a synthesis of ongoing and recently completed initiatives that align with circular economy principles and support low-emission, resource efficient agricultural systems in Small Island Developing States (SIDS).
A flagship project, supported by the Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO), investigates the valorization of post-consumer food waste through the mass rearing of the Black Soldier Fly (Hermetia illucens). The larvae are cultivated using segregated fruit and vegetable waste, with ongoing efforts to include institutional food waste streams such as those from university cafeterias. The resulting high protein larvae biomass is being evaluated as a sustainable alternative to soybean meal in broiler diets. Parallel studies are assessing its impact on microbial safety, carcass quality, and consumer acceptability, thereby demonstrating the feasibility of insect farming as a scalable, circular bioeconomy model for the Caribbean.
Complementary to this are vermicomposting initiatives which explore the decomposition of kitchen scraps and shredded paper using Eisenia fetida (red wiggler worms). These studies not only quantify vermicast yield and nutrient quality under different substrate ratios but also investigates the socio educational implications of promoting household-level waste recycling and its integration into urban agriculture systems.
A third initiative addresses poultry waste management through the production and utilization of poultry litter-derived biochar. Biochar is generated via slow pyrolysis of manure from intensive poultry operations and is currently being evaluated for its efficacy in improving soil physicochemical properties, enhancing nutrient use efficiency, and sequestering carbon. Field trials across vegetable cropping systems are supported by emissions profiling and life cycle assessment to determine the role of biochar in climate-smart agriculture and greenhouse gas mitigation.
Central to these waste valorisation strategies is the role of microorganisms in the decomposition of organic substrates. Microbial communities drive critical processes such as nutrient cycling and degradation. Previous research utilizing deep sequencing, not only provided unprecedented depth of microbial dynamics during the composting process but also identified fifteen new pathogens of biosafety concern. Future research will focus on the identification and monitoring of pathogenic organisms in composts to ensure that end-products meet biosecurity standards, safeguarding both human and environmental health.
Collectively, these initiatives underscore the Department s commitment to applied, interdisciplinary research that advances national and regional priorities in organic waste valorization, soil health restoration, and climate resilience. The integration of insect bioconversion, vermiculture, and biochar technologies highlights the potential for synergistic, locally adapted waste management models that contribute to more sustainable and regenerative food systems in the Caribbean.
title: From Waste to Worth: Harnessing Organic Waste Streams for Agricultural Sustainability and Climate Resilience

description: The Caribbean faces an escalating crisis from massive Sargassum seaweed influxes reaching over 800 dump trucks per day on some beaches in Barbados in 2022. Rum and Sargassum Inc., a University of the West Indies spin-off, is transforming this environmental hazard into a renewable energy opportunity. By combining Sargassum with rum distillery wastewater and other local waste streams, the company produces biomethane fuel capable of powering conventional vehicles, supplying the national gas grid, and even supporting electricity and LNG exports. Since 2019, the team has advanced from lab-scale experiments to the landmark  Test Drive Zero  pilot demonstration, fueling vehicles with biogas in 2024, and recently secured $1.1M USD for a compressed natural gas (CNG) sales pilot in Barbados to expand adoption. The initiative is further strengthened by the development of a Sargassum Biomass Prediction App (SBPA), which integrates satellite data and drone-derived biomass estimations to guide harvesting and supply chain planning. This talk highlights the technical, and entrepreneurial innovations driving Rum and Sargassum Inc., and demonstrates how a Caribbean-led solution can deliver scalable climate resilience, sustainable transport, and global biofuel leadership.
title: Natural Gas from Renewable Local Waste

description:
In the past decade, Trinidad and Tobago have experienced rapid urbanisation. The development is characterised by a low population growth rate but a disproportionate increase in urbanised land. One of the reasons is that the islands experience a dispersed urban sprawl with a spread of low-density urban settlements. This pattern has resulted in a higher built-up area per capita compared to other CSIDS (ECLAC, 2024). At the same time, the islands have a high percentage of informal settlements. The twin-island state has one of the highest percentages of its population living in slums, 24.7%, after
Haiti (74.4%), Jamaica (60.5%), and Guyana (33.1%) (UN-Habitat, 2020). The presentation will highlight the SDG at the city level and will give insights from four pilot studies that provide valuable insights to address and plan for SDG11: Sustainable cities and communities.
title: Making Homes-Planning Sustainable Cities

description:
Globally, cities and communities continue to face progressively intensifying pressure associated with unsustainable consumption and production, particularly in the context of Small Island Developing States, where there is a linkage between high levels of urbanization and limited resources. This has resulted in the need to apply innovative urban planning principles in order to harness the potential of communities and assist them in overcoming challenges of waste generation, which are exacerbated by traditional linear practices. The concept of the circular economy (CE) within cities has gained
traction globally as a viable option to the prevailing linear economic model within the Caribbean, by removing the dependency of economic success on the availability of resources. However, in the unique context of these Small Island Developing States (SIDS) where a high level of informality and enduring legacy colonial elements persist, a scale mismatch exists where current global frameworks do not cater for the data shortages, underdeveloped infrastructure and the unique SIDS governance structures present within the Caribbean region.
This research seeks to address this gap by investigating the capacity of communities in Caribbean SIDS to function as circular municipalities in the context of solid waste management and material recovery/reuse, through development of an assessment framework which incorporates technical circular economy indicators with less studied social community-level indicators as key determinants of circular capacity. A case study approach was taken, assessing municipalities such as the informal community of East Port of Spain within Trinidad and Tobago, which provided a diverse range of results on
how formal and informal settlements mobilize knowledge and practices related to circularity, and why a tailored assessment framework is required to accurately assess and support successful and inclusive CE progressions across the unique region.
title: A FRAMEWORK FOR ASSESSING THE CIRCULAR CAPACITY OF COMMUNITIES WITHIN CARIBBEAN SMALL ISLAND DEVELOPING STATES (SIDS): A CASE STUDY OF TRINIDAD AND TOBAGO

description:
Concrete is the most widely used man-made material globally, contributing about 8% of anthropogenic CO? emissions. For every tonne of cement produced, approximately 0.9 tonnes of CO? are released. This study explores eco-material alternatives for Small Island Developing States (SIDS), with a focus on Trinidad and Tobago and its locally available resources. Investigated options include recycled aggregate concrete (RCA), blended cements with sargassum and mud volcano materials, and direct carbon capture. RCA was examined in detail as indicate that smart demolition is the most effective
strategy for maintaining strength and a sustainable alternative to demolition waste disposal, supporting the circular economy. In its absence, treatments such as acid washing and silica fume slurry can partially restore RCA performance. However, acid treatment may damage the RCA mortar bond and increase long-term shrinkage. This research demonstrates viable low-carbon pathways for concrete production in SIDS while highlighting some critical challenges for durability and performance. (SDGs: 9, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15)
title: Ecocrete: The Sustainable Concrete Alternative

description: Teachers are integral to Education for Sustainable Development (ESD) given their first-hand interaction with students at all levels of the education system, and their potential to facilitate ESD competencies through its infusion into different subject areas and the pedagogies that they utilise in the classrooms. With this in mind, the ESD Working Group within the School of Education at The UWI Mona Campus comprises a multidisciplinary group of teacher educators focused on awareness raising, capacity-building and outreach amongst pre- and in-service teachers, as well as associated research to further practice in this area. This presentation will offer an overview of the Group and some highlights on their work in the areas of awareness raising, capacity-building and research to highlight how collaborative efforts such as these can further research and practice in ESD in teacher education.
title: The Education for Sustainable Development (ESD) Working Group: Furthering Research and Practice in Teacher Education

description:
Mainstreaming sustainability into higher education is not optional, it is central to the survival, resilience, and relevance of institutions in the 21st century. Higher Education Institutions (HEIs) must do more than produce graduates; they must cultivate a sustainability mindset that equips students with the knowledge, values, and agency to navigate and lead in a climate-impacted world. In doing so, they advance the UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), particularly SDG 4 (Quality Education), SDG 16 (Strong Institutions), and SDG 17 (Partnerships). This presentation draws on the experience of The University of the West Indies (UWI), where the One UWI Responsible Futures Programme was established as a regional first. Adapted from international frameworks of the International Association of Universities (IAU) and Students Organising for Sustainability UK (SOS-UK), the programme was re-designed to reflect the Caribbean reality of small island developing states on the climate frontlines. The result is a Caribbean-grown model for sustainability integration that can also be tailored for secondary and even primary institutions.
Three critical lessons underpin this work: leadership must be visible and consistent; governance structures must embed sustainability institutionally; and students must be empowered as partners and co-creators, not passive beneficiaries. Through initiatives such as the ratification of a university-wide Sustainability Policy, a co-created student Sustainability Strategy, annual student audits, and groundbreaking actions like the University-wide Sustainability Teach-In, UWI has demonstrated that student-led, leadership-supported programmes can transform institutional culture. Recognition has been global, with UWI ranking among the top institutions worldwide for student engagement in sustainability. The key takeaway is clear: sustainability integration must be strategic, meaningful, and collaborative, while tailored to institutional context. For HEIs, mainstreaming sustainability is not just a programme, it is a long-term investment in student agency, curriculum relevance, institutional resilience, and global credibility. The institutions that embrace it will not only endure but lead.
title: Mainstreaming Sustainability into HEIs: Advancing Resilient Futures through a whole institution approach

description:
Solid waste management (SWM) in Caribbean Small Island Developing States has evolved dramatically since the late 1980s from manual collection on unpaved roads and inappropriate storage containers to targeted bans on single-use plastics and expanded deposit-refund systems. This presentation traces that history through vivid case studies: strained curbside services in 1987, rampant illegal dumping and coastal pollution by 1990, early reinforcement of derelict vehicles as artificial reefs, and the OECS Solid Waste Management Project s pilot cruise-ship waste fee. 
Contemporary highlights include Barbados  long-running Returnable Container Act, Trinidad and Tobago s iCARE recycling initiative, and the RePlast OECS voucher-based scheme in Saint Lucia. A survey of CARICOM states reveals wide variation in legislative frameworks Public Health and Litter Acts, styrofoam bans, statutory waste authorities and stark dependence on landfills nearing capacity. Fragmented policies, weak enforcement, and treaty ratifications without domestic follow-through underscore governance and institutional bottlenecks.
The presentation frames the circular-economy puzzle for Caribbean SIDS: design-out-waste principles clash with scant on-island processing, and small market scales thwart economical recycling and regeneration loops. Community engagement emerges as a bright spot private-sector partnerships, eco-resort initiatives, and household incentive programmes but inconsistent public policy dampens broader uptake. 
Financing realities are equally challenging: recycling ventures hinge on subsidies, donor grants, and limited deposit-return schemes, while transboundary recycling markets expose the region to shipping costs and regulatory risks. 
Concluding with  solutions beyond technology,  the session argues for enforceable legislation, coherent institutional mandates, sustainable financing models (levies, EPR schemes, PPPs), and deeper regional collaboration. By situating SWM within broader climate resilience and circular-economy frameworks, Caribbean states can shift from disposal-centric systems toward resilient, inclusive waste futures.
title: Towards Sustainable Solid Waste Management in the Caribbean

description: Plastic pollution remains one of the most pressing global challenges, threatening ecosystems, human health, and the long-term sustainability of modern societies. At the same time, the Caribbean and Latin America have faced the recurring inundation of Sargassum natans, a brown seaweed that has accumulated along coastlines for more than a decade, disrupting marine life, tourism, and coastal economies. These two issues plastic waste and uncontrolled Sargassum blooms represent parallel environmental crises that demand innovative solutions. By recognising the alginate-rich composition of Sargassum, this research positions the seaweed not as a nuisance but as a valuable resource for bio-based plastic alternatives. Through its valorisation into biodegradable alginate composites, the study directly addresses both problems: reducing reliance on fossil-derived plastics while providing a sustainable pathway for utilling Sargassum biomass. This presentation will outline ongoing research focused on the extraction, optimization, and valorisation of alginate from Sargassum. It will highlight the use of advanced extraction and statistical optimization techniques to enhance yield and purity, the fabrication of alginate-based films, and the application of composite strategies to overcome limitations in mechanical and barrier properties. Additionally, the talk will discuss techno-economic perspectives on the feasibility of large-scale production, with particular attention to sustainability, cost competitiveness, and alignment with circular economy goals. Ultimately, the presentation will demonstrate the potential of Sargassum-derived bioplastics to reduce reliance on synthetic plastics, mitigate the environmental burden of Sargassum blooms, and contribute to sustainable packaging innovation in the Caribbean and beyond. Ultimately, this study demonstrates how Sargassumderived bioplastics can foster industry innovation (SDG 9), promote responsible consumption and production (SDG 12), support climate action through reduced carbon impacts (SDG 13), and protect life below water by mitigating plastic and seaweed pollution (SDG 14).
title: Turning the Tide: Sargassum Valorisation for Sustainable Bioplastic Innovation

description:
The (primary) processing of poultry leads to the production of inedible by-products such as eviscera and feathers. The industry generates large quantities of waste worldwide for which disposal is difficult. Based on this, valorisation of the inedible by-products (with emphasis on feather waste) has become a new and emerging area of study, especially given that raw feathers are relatively insoluble and have a low digestibility. This presentation briefly outlines the potential opportunities for the incorporation of raw feather particles and powder into a range of applications including composite building materials,
asphalt modifiers and biofertilizers, as well as outline the current research work being done on the extraction of keratin protein from feathers. Successful extraction of keratin can lead to the further conversion into a number of high-value bio-products such as biodegradable plastics, packaging trays and polymeric composite materials.
United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) #9 Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure   Build resilient infrastructure and promote innovation
title: Valorisation of Poultry Feathers

description: Caribbean coasts are subjected to natural and anthropogenic hazards that can cause significant flooding and erosion with concomitant adverse consequences that can substantially hamper developmental and sustainability goals. In order to combat these disastrous events, given the uncertainty of the future meteorological and oceanographic climate, regional engineers rely on a variety of solutions including hard, soft and hybrid engineering approaches. However, engineered solutions implemented without the environment and people at its core, cannot allow for Caribbean coasts to become resilient- surviving and recovering from the damaging impacts of climate change and other drivers. As such, there has been an augmented focus regionally to explore engineered designs that ably balance biodiversity goals, integrating local communities and other key stakeholders, providing the expected levels of protection over the design life and yet cost-effective to optimize limited financial resources (i.e., achieving SDG3, SDG8, SDG9, SDG11, SDG13, SDG14 & SDG17).
title: Engineering our Caribbean Coasts: Challenges to Coastal Resillience

description:
As climate change intensifies coastal hazards, traditional hard infrastructure is proving increasingly inadequate, particularly in Small Island Developing States where resources are limited and exposure to flooding is high. Nature-based solutions act as physical barriers and offer hydrodynamic buffering, attenuating wave energy, reducing storm surge heights, and trapping sediment to lower flood depths. However, their effectiveness in mitigating flooding under compound conditions involving storm surge, rainfall, and river discharge remains insufficiently quantified.
This research combines laboratory experiments and numerical modeling to investigate how the ecological characteristics of nature-based solutions influence flood behaviour. These insights are used to incorporate nature-based systems into a probabilistic compound flood modeling framework that accounts for uncertainty in climate conditions, flood drivers, and ecosystem performance.
The study supports SDGs 13, 11, 14, and 15 by strengthening climate resilience and protecting ecosystems, contributes to SDGs 1 and 8 through the preservation of livelihoods and natural resources, and advances SDG 9 by promoting innovative, resilient infrastructure that integrates natural and engineered defenses for the Caribbean.
title: Leveraging Nature-based Solutions to Achieve Coastal Resilience in the Caribbean

description: This study seeks to examine risk factors for engagement in drug and alcohol usage among school youth in the Caribbean. Data was derived from a survey which was administered to Form 4 and 5 school youth in St. Vincent and the Grenadines (N = 1491), Tobago (N = 777) and St. Kitts and Nevis (N = 767) in 2023. Twenty-six risk factors in the individual, family, peer, school and community domains were examined. Ordinary least squares regression was used to examine the relationship between the risk factors and drug and alcohol usage. The study provides estimates of the level of drug and alcohol usage among school youths and revealed that the most important predictors were the intention to use drugs, low self-control, having antisocial peers, experiencing family abuse and experiencing family problems. The findings were discussed in the context of interventions aimed at reducing drug and alcohol usage among young persons.
title: Risk Factors for Drug and Alcohol Usage Among School Youth in the Caribbean

description:
Gangs and gang-related violence continue to pose a serious threat to public safety in Trinidad and Tobago, with juveniles increasingly vulnerable to recruitment and involvement. Media reports have underscored a troubling trend juvenile gang membership appears to be occurring at progressively younger ages. This study investigates the principal risk domains and associated factors influencing juvenile gang involvement, utilizing secondary data from the Trinidad and Tobago Youth Survey conducted in 2006. The research aimed to identify the key risk domains and determine which domain(s) demonstrated the strongest association with juvenile affiliation in adult gangs. It also hypothesized that the presence of a higher number of risk factors would correspond to an increased likelihood of gang involvement.
The study was guided by interactional theory, which offers a comprehensive framework for understanding the interplay between individual behaviour, weakened social bonds, and environmental influences, while also accounting for structural and interpersonal dynamics. As this research relied on secondary data, the original methodology was reviewed and found to have employed purposive sampling. The resulting sample was comprised of 2,206 third and fifth form students from high-risk secondary schools across Trinidad and Tobago.
The survey instrument examined thirty risk factors distributed across five domains: family, peer, school, community, and individual. The data were analyzed using a range of statistical tests in SPSS. The findings, disaggregated by gang status, revealed that 80.1% of respondents had never been involved in a gang, while 7.5% identified as gang associates, 6.5% as former gang members, and 6.0% as current gang members.
The analysis indicated that risk factors within the peer domain particularly association with antisocial peers and the receipt of rewards for antisocial behaviour were significantly correlated with gang involvement. Additional contributing factors included perceived handgun availability, social disorganization, and early engagement in antisocial behaviour.
Furthermore, the likelihood of gang involvement was found to increase with cumulative exposure to multiple risk factors. The study reinforces the need for evidence-based prevention strategies, articularly those grounded in the public health model, and advocates for targeted, tertiary-level interventions aimed at high-risk youth. It is evident that continuous research should be conducted to monitor shifting patterns and emergent risks.
title: An Examination of Risk Factors for Juvenile Involvement in Adult-Gangs in Trinidad and Tobago

description:
The Youth Art in Times of COVID-19 project, part of the Caribbean Alliance of National Psychological Associations Caribbean Youth Mental Health Champions Project, sought to provide a creative outlet for Caribbean children to tell the stories of their lived experiences during the COVID-19 pandemic. The psychosocial impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on children has been extensively researched in international settings but there is a paucity of comparable studies of Caribbean participants. This study sought to make a unique contribution to knowledge in this area with the aim of better understanding what mental health and psychosocial support (MHPSS) interventions may best support the region's children. It also sought to make a methodological contribution to knowledge generation in the region by using non-traditional research techniques, thereby indigenizing the findings to a greater extent.
The data were based on the visual artwork submissions of 103 Caribbean children. Art pieces were solicited via social media platforms and direct contact with youth groups. Submissions were screened for suitability prior to inclusion in the analysis for the study. Utilizing a participatory arts-based research methodology, the data comprised both the artwork as well as the artists  own descriptions of their art. Thematic analysis, using the process outlined by Braun and Clarke (2006) was used to identify latent themes in the art pieces and artists  descriptions. An emphasis was placed on identifying latent themes based on symbols, words and descriptions rather than semantic themes based on the artists  choice of materials or composition technique.
The analysis identified seven major themes including: 1. Being socially and physically isolated, 2. Having strong emotional reactions, 3. Feeling overwhelmed, 4. COVID-19 virus as a threat, 5. Pandemic-related health literacy, 6. Attempts at learning continuity, and 7. Signs of a hopeful future. The prevalence of the theme of social isolation was pronounced. The implications of this research are that further support may be required for children in the area of disaster mental health and climate change resilience which is an underserved area in Caribbean youth development, education and psychology. The implications for further research are far-reaching and signal a pressing need. Of particular research interest is children s use of art as a form of  self-therapy  in times of distress.
This study makes a valuable contribution to knowledge across the areas of youth development, education and psychology by identifying areas for action that may enhance youths  ability to be resilient and cope with climate change and health and education related crises such as those caused by the 2020-2022 global pandemic. It is hoped that these insights will be used to inform tailored mental health and psychosocial support interventions for Caribbean children.
title: Thematic Analysis of Caribbean Children's Art During the COVID-19 Pandemic

description:
Research on lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender and queer (LGBTQ) issues is critical to raising awareness about and building support for the community and bolstering advocacy for policy and legislative change. There is a dearth of resources and limited capacity in countries where homophobia and transphobia are pervasive to undertake research. Consequently, LGBTQ-related research is often conducted using narrow frames which focus on narratives around violence, criminalization and HIV and by individuals who sometimes rarely have connection to the country or community they are investigating. The challenges with conducting research in the Caribbean are compounded by limited sampling frame and difficulties recruiting research participants due to the antipathy that exists. Therefore, it is difficult to conduct quantitative research and produce results that are reliable, valid and generalizable. Consequently, the LGBTQ community is often pathologized and the research is not always representative of the realities and needs due to bias, lack of partnership with LGBTQ groups and/or involvement of LGBTQ people and failure to acknowledge their epistemological standpoints. Community organisers do not all have the wherewithal to understand and use research effectively. For example, articles published in peer reviewed journals are not generally written in accessible language for those outside the academy and some activists lack the capacity to use the findings from academic research to bolster their advocacy and programmes. Consequently, given the challenges that exist in this regard  and the lack of partnership between both communities (i.e. community organisers and academics), research findings are often ignored and/or not used.
This paper presents data on the situation in the Caribbean based on experiential learning, a rapid assessment among LGBTQ leaders in the region and secondary data analysis which illuminate the challenges and opportunities to develop local expertise on the issues affecting the LGBTQ community through robust research and publishing data that would not only raise awareness about the realities and needs of the community but decolonize knowledge production around such a critical issue as well as challenge the narratives about the Caribbean LGBTQ community.
title: Mind the Gap: Indigenizing LGBTQ Research to Redress Epistemological Violence in the Caribbean

description:
Introduction: Andrea Bharrat, a 23-year-old woman, was murdered in Trinidad and Tobago in 2021. Bharatt s murder triggered a massive public response, including the closing of businesses, candlelight vigils, protests and extensive media coverage. Numerous letters to newspaper editors were written. This study uses Critical Discourse Analysis (CDA) to investigate gender ideologies underlying dominant and hidden narratives within said letters to newspaper editors.
Objectives: The objectives of this research are: (1) to investigate the dominant narratives within the collection of letters to newspaper editors, (2) to uncover the hidden narratives in the collection, and (3) to explore what these dominant and hidden narratives reveal about underlying gender ideologies and gender or power relations.
Methodology: Critical Discourse Analysis (CDA) is utilized to investigate the letters to the editor for three major newspapers in Trinidad and Tobago   the Trinidad and Tobago Guardian, the Trinidad Express and the Trinidad and Tobago Newsday. 213 letters from February to April 2021 were examined. Language choices were interrogated to reveal the assumptions and ideologies underlying the public discourse.
Discussion: This project engages the theme of Caribbean intellectual, philosophical and everyday thought, together with that of activism and solidarity. The discourse represented via the letters expresses solidarity with other forms of activism, such as marches and vigils, in the wake of Bharatt s murder. This medium of everyday thought is systematically analyzed via a gender lens to reveal philosophical ideologies, that is, underlying belief systems upheld by individuals and collectives.
Results: The study uncovered seven key ideological narratives: (1) Toxic Masculinity; (2) Perpetrators as Monsters; (3) Punishment; (4) Protection; (5) Respectability; (6) Victim-Blaming and (7) Citizenship. The elevation of Bharatt s status from daughter to martyr is also traced. Letters to the editor, as the selected media, proved to be a rich space for discourse   both resistances to existing patriarchal power relations as well as the resultant masculinist backlash. The results demonstrate that ideologies can mask the way public discourse sustains gendered power relations in Trinidad and Tobago.
Keywords: Critical Discourse Analysis, Andrea Bharratt, Newspapers, Letters to the Editor, Resistances to Patriarchy, Masculinist Backlash
title: Dear Editor: A Study of Gender Ideology Through Newspaper Discourse

description:
In December 2024 over 98 states and organisations appeared before the International Court of Justice to give oral statements in support of their request for an Advisory Opinion on the legal responsibilities of states for climate change. This initiative was spearheaded by small island developing states (SIDS) from the Pacific and the Caribbean. This study is framed around the theory of coloniality, which understands colonialism as a system of organising life that has persisted long after its formal end. This study relies on the climate coloniality lens of geographer Farhana Sultana and coloniality/decoloniality discourses of Latin American and Caribbean thinkers to frame the slowness and inadequacy of climate action and help explain the sudden flurry of climate litigation initiatives. This study aims to explain and highlight the prominence not simply of SIDs but also of the world views of Indigenous Peoples in SIDS in the arguments made in this climate litigation initiative. The paper uses a decoloniality framework to re-read the  vulnerability  and 'stewardship' discourses which constructivist theories of international relations have used to refer to SIDS and Indigenous Peoples in SIDS. Decolonial notions of  ontological disobedience  (Burman 2017) and 'shifting the geography of reason' (Gordon 2011) will be applied as a frame or understanding SIDS  decision to transition from the accepted and now ineffective sites of multilateral climate action, the UN Framework Convention on Climate Change and its annual Conference of the Parties (COP) negotiations, to international courts and tribunals.
Methodologically, this study takes up the decolonial approaches of moving outside of coloniality s imposition of compartmentalised academic disciplines by engaging in 'just thinking' (Mignolo 2007). This approach, incorporates Sultana s practice of 'feeling with climate': incorporating emotional responses to climate crisis (as opposed to the climate crisis), action and activism as part of the evidence to be factored into a decolonial analysis. 
Keywords: coloniality, decoloniality, climate coloniality, advisory opinion, climate change, climate action, UNFCCC, COP, SIDS, Indigenous People, self-determination, pluriversality, ontological disobedience, epistemic disobedience, shifting the geography of reason
title: Ontological Disobedience: Pluriversal Perspectives in the International Court of Justice Advisory Opinion on Climate Change

description:
Who is the Caribbean woman?
If we listen to her portrayal in regional music, she is worshipped for the rhythm of her hips, the sway of her walk and her ability to please her man   a trope immortalised in David Rudder s Bacchanal Lady where the Caribbean woman is both celebrated and confined to her body s performance:  the sweet scandal when she walk, the real magic when she talk  and his desire for her to  make some love to me . But is that all she is?
Caribbean women navigate systems that demand peak performance   both professionally and emotionally   while denying structural support for their wellbeing, sexual and economic autonomy or healing. This paper exposes how the commodification of Caribbean women and girls, historically rooted in slavery and colonialism, persists in contemporary workplaces and intimate settings. It introduces Diddy-Ideology   a conceptual framework for understanding extractive gendered labour through three interlinked dimensions:
1. Institutional silence - The suppression of grievances (harassment, maternity inequality), masked by gratitude rhetoric;
2. Emotional Endurance - The normalization of emotional pain, framed as strength (e.g. enduring infidelity for family stability);
3. Dual servitude   The unpaid emotional labour of  office moms , caregiving and sexual obligations in private life and performative joy in public/social spaces
Objectives: The paper investigates the intertwined commodification of Caribbean women in labour and love under neocolonial systems. It aims to:
1. Trace historical continuities between enslaved women s exploitation and modern institutional neglect of caregiving, health and autonomy
2. Propose decolonial policy and cultural interventions for gender justice in both private and public domains.
Methodology: Guided by a decolonial feminist theoretical lens, this conceptual exploratory study draws on qualitative media analysis, Caribbean feminist scholarship and social media discourse. Focusing on platforms such as Twitter/X, TikTok and Facebook, the study examines how Caribbean users engage with narratives around male power, fame, accountability and the normalization of abuse. The recent P.Diddy/RICO case serves as a cultural reference point for analysing gender scripts in music, religion and family expectations. Workplace conditions are explored through HR policy reviews and historical archival analysis of colonial labour codes.
Findings (Preliminary Insights): This conceptual discourse analysis yields exploratory insights intended to guide future empirical research. It identifies key cultural patterns that warrant deeper investigation. Despite increasing representation in the workforce, Caribbean women operate in institutions that echo pre-colonial/colonial labour models   where biological, emotional, psychological and reproductive realities are rendered invisible. Simultaneously, they are socialised to perform love as labour, exchanging compliance and silence for stability and status. These dual domains reflect a shared cultural economy where women are expected to function flawlessly, give endlessly and endure without visible resistance.
Conclusion and Praxis Recommendations: The paper proposes a decolonial gender justice framework including:
1. Legislated care-oriented HR policies (e.g.: menstrual leave and public childcare support)
2. Gender-sensitive workplace design standards
3. School curricula teaching critical analysis of romanticised exploitation
By bridging history, digital ethnography and feminist praxis, this work reimagines a Caribbean economic future that recognises women not as commodities, but as full human beings. Diddy-Ideology offers a critical tool for dismantling systemic extraction in both intimate and institutional spaces.
title: Bacchanal Lady: Love, Labour and the Legacy of Control: Diddy Ideology and the commodification of Caribbean women in work and intimacy

description:
Brief Introduction to the Research Project: Cancer is a leading cause of death in Latin America and the Caribbean, and the second leading cause of death in Trinidad and Tobago. Common cancer treatments such as surgery, chemo- and radiation therapy can result in side effects like malnutrition and nutrition problems. One of the nutritional problems experienced during cancer treatment is nutritional impact symptoms (NIS). A review of the related literature has highlighted the role of good nutrition for improved cancer related health outcomes and the impact of NIS on the nutritional status of cancer patients. Given the limited research in this area within the Caribbean, we conducted this study.
Objective(s): To examine the NIS and dietary changes experienced by adults living with cancer (ALWC) in Trinidad and the nutritional support and guidance they receive during treatment.
Method: A qualitative study was conducted with 12 ALWC. They were recruited from a nonprofit organization (NPO) provided that they were diagnosed within the last five years and undergoing treatment. All potential participants were identified by an NPO liaison and researchers obtained signed informed consent after the purpose of the study was explained. Participants  experiences with NIS and dietary changes were examined using face-to-face interviews guided by 21 semi-structured questions. All interviews were recorded, transcribed and analysed thematically using Dedoose  software.
Results: Twelve female participants were interviewed, from which three major themes emerged: Nutrition Impact Symptoms (NIS), Dietary Changes, and Lack of Information. Participants experienced a combination of symptoms during treatment: nausea, metallic taste, constipation, fatigue, and loss of appetite, taste and smell. Dietary changes included the introduction of new foods and the decrease or eliminations of others, changes in portion sizes and meal frequency. Intake restrictions were also noted for flour, sugar, processed foods, artificially flavoured foods/beverages, and meats (chicken, beef and pork). Participants reported receiving limited information and dietary guidance from healthcare professionals. They relied on personal research, information from other cancer patients and support groups.
Conclusion: These findings suggest the potential value of capacity-building initiatives that encourage healthcare professionals to incorporate appropriate nutritional guidance in cancer care. Additionally, the integration of dietary education into public health efforts may help support or empower patients and caregivers to make more informed nutrition-related decisions during treatment.
title: Nutritional Impact Symptoms and Dietary Changes Among Women in a Cancer Support Network: A Qualitative Analysis

description:
Background: Between 1992 and 2005, fierce pro-abortion liberalisation movements were active: the Pro-Reform Group in Guyana and Advocates for Safe Parenthood: Improving Reproductive Equity (ASPIRE) in Trinidad and Tobago. Ultimately, Guyana passed its Medical Termination of Pregnancy Act in 1995, becoming the only English-speaking Caribbean state to date to legalise elective abortion; meanwhile, Trinidad and Tobago still criminalises abortion in most cases. To examine these different outcomes in highly culturally similar nations, I examine parliamentary debates and news articles from each country during the years specified. I find religious composition, maternal mortality rates and economic strength as important explanatory factors. In an era of religious nationalism, rollback of women s reproductive rights and continuous economic crises, I ask: how did religion, neoliberal economics and maternal mortality unite or divide citizens and the state during the abortion rights debates of the 1990s-2000s in Guyana and Trinidad and Tobago?
Objective(s): To test different factors which may have contributed to this divergence in policy in the two countries. I tested three factors: maternal mortality rates, gender makeup of parliament and religious composition in both countries Methods/Methodology: I utilised process tracing to understand the process by which the law was changed or failed to change in each context, comprising of a series of  hoop  (yes/no) tests at key stages (see Marek Nacyzk s work to learn more).
Results: The maternal mortality hypothesis, that indicated that maternal mortality was the primary explanatory factor for the divergence, was proven correct while the gender and religious hypothesis were disproven and inconclusive, respectively. An interesting side-finding was the role of the Muslim community in supporting the Catholic Church s antichoice rhetoric, an idea which has not been duly explored in the literature.
I will discuss how globalized, neoliberal legacies of colonialism set postcolonial states up for maternal health crises, and how this interacts with local and global politics of women s reproductive rights in heterogeneously religious countries in the Global South. In doing so I engage literatures of geographies of religion and the body, Caribbean feminist and intersectional work and feminist theories of health in postcolonial states.
Importantly, I also interrogate the positions and rhetorics of different religious groups in both countries towards women s sexuality and reproductive autonomy, examining how the abortion debate served as a site of patriarchal collusion between Muslim and Christian/Catholic interests and the unifying power of conservative religious politics.
title: Can't Afford to 'Slip and Fall': A Process Tracing of Abortion Liberalisation Attempts in Trinidad and Tobago and Guyana, 1992-2005

description:
Sex education is arguably one of the most debatable  taboo  topics, in the education fraternity, both locally and within the Caribbean region. Since the discovery of the Human Immune-deficiency Virus (HIV) and the Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome (AIDS) in the 1980s, Koop (1986) recommended that young people be made aware of heterosexual and homosexual relationships and its impact on the spread of this deadly disease. Consequently, the Caribbean Community (CARICOM) was forced to develop and implement policies with the thrust of reshaping values and promoting positive behaviours in youths, as one of its major objectives, for arresting the alarming and rapid spread of HIV/AIDS in the region.
The result of this initiative was a resolution passed in 1994 by the CARICOM Standing Committee of Ministers of Education to implement Health and Family Life Education (HFLE), in schools throughout the region. Subsequently, an evaluation of the HFLE curriculum, at secondary schools in Trinidad and Tobago, alike most of the Caribbean region, highlighted several gaps in both the content and delivery of the sexuality component (UNFPA (2022 p.10-11), including but not limited to the lack of work on vulnerable and marginalized youths and the absence of curriculum reform to address some of the challenges related to sex and sexuality within educational systems. As such, the objectives of this research were to determine if there is a need for CSE at the secondary school level and what are the anticipated benefits and challenges.
Teachers, as one of the most important stakeholders in education, are held in high esteem by students and therefore play a critical and imperative role in their development. They are viewed as knowledgeable, reliable and credible, and in most cases trusted by students (Rosenthal & Smith, 1995; Harrison & Dempsey, 1998).
It is through this lens that this research was conducted, using a case study approach, with a qualitative method, that interrogated teachers on the inclusion of CSE programs in the secondary schools' curriculum. This descriptive  case study , was examined within understanding of a 'bounded system', that is the investigation of a program/phenomenon at a particular time and place (Merriam, 1998), which involved the perspectives of six teachers, of varied characteristics, within a specific government secondary school.
The findings revealed that teachers agreed with the existing gaps in HFLE, identifying that age appropriate and relevant topics on STIs, LGBTQI+ and relationships are critical aspects of any sexuality education program. Teachers indicated that they are willing and will be comfortable teaching CSE, once provided with the necessary training and resources. However, they anticipate resistance from the Ministry of Education, parents and religious institutions. They also suggested the need for lateral research, including all stakeholders, citing the importance of collaboration and advocacy on the issue of CSE. These findings emerged as critical components to the development and implementation of policies, as intervention strategies are required to address the fluidity of sexual modernity.
title: Teachers' Perspective of Comprehensive Sexuality Education (CSE) in the Secondary Schools' Curriculum

description:
In 2020, the Government of Trinidad and Tobago imposed restrictions to reduce the spread of the COVID-19 virus, including the shift from in-person schooling to remote learning. Many parents were impacted by this change since they were then required to provide additional support to their children s learning while managing the social, economic, and psychological impacts of the disease. This study focused on understanding how parents navigated their primary school child(ren)'s remote learning during the COVID-19 pandemic. The study explored how parents viewed this transition to remote learning, the challenges they experienced, and the strategies they employed to cope with the changes derived from their child(ren) s new way of learning, while balancing what was a crisis for many.
This study used a qualitative research methodology and a phenomenological research design and was framed using the Family Adjustment and Adaptation Response (FAAR) Model. Purposeful sampling was used to recruit ten (10) parents of children who attended primary school before the pandemic and transitioned from in-person learning to remote learning. Data was analysed using thematic analysis. 
Findings revealed that the COVID-19 pandemic presented various stressors to the families and various factors affected how parents coped, including the number of children at home, the age of the children, the social support systems available, the parent-teacher relationship, the parents  work schedule, and the finances available to them. The study also found that some families adjusted to the demands of the pandemic and responded in ways that resulted in the transition being beneficial to the family unit. Parents who identified as having greater levels of financial stability were more resilient and were able to adjust more easily to the demands of remote schooling for their children. This study is significant because it demonstrates both the challenges and parents  creative strategies used to strengthen their child(ren) s remote learning, and provide social workers, teachers, and policy makers with insights into the institutional and structural changes needed to support the on-going demands placed on parents to manage online teaching strategies in a postpandemic world.
title: Understanding how Parents Navigated their Primary School Children's Remote Learning during the COVID-19 Pandemic

description:
'Some wounds are inherited in silence.' As a peace educator and co-parenting coach from Trinidad and Tobago, this truth shaped my personal and professional journey. My potential doctoral research, introduces Sankofa Serenity a 12-week trauma healing and conflict
transformation model developed to address the emotional, spiritual, and relational impacts of intergenerational trauma on African-descended families. Rooted in the philosophies of Sankofa (to return and retrieve ancestral wisdom) and Jegnaship (intergenerational
mentorship), this praxis-driven model fuses Afrocentric thought with trauma theory to help co-parents heal out loud.
Objectives: This research project aims to:
Design and evaluate an Afrocentric trauma-healing intervention model for high-conflict co-parents.
Assess changes in emotional regulation, communication, and generational identity across pilot participants.
Bridge ancestral Caribbean epistemologies and post-traumatic growth theory within a community-based participatory framework.
Methodology: Using a mixed-methods, action research design, the study will combine:
Qualitative interviews and storytelling from co-parents and facilitators
Quantitative pre- and post-assessments measuring emotional and relational shifts
Participant journaling and facilitated healing circles as data sources
Analysis will involve NVivo for thematic coding and SPSS for tracking behavioural changes.
Expected Outcomes: The proposed pilot implementation of Sankofa Serenity is expected to enhance co-parenting dynamics, through enhanced emotional regulation, improved communication and deeper awareness of inherited patterns of trauma. The study anticipates that participants will report a shift toward more intentional, peace-centered parenting. Findings will be shared during the 2025 conference period, contributing to regional dialogue on trauma recovery and culturally rooted family interventions.
Relevance to Conference Theme: This presentation engages directly with the conference s focus on Caribbean thought, research, and praxis. Sankofa Serenity offers a decolonial, action-oriented intervention that emerges from Caribbean realities and responds to generational cycles of silence, conflict, and disconnection. The model centers legacy restoration, emotional literacy, and ancestral wisdom as tools of both personal and societal healing. By positioning co-parenting as a site of resistance and resilience, the research contributes to emerging Caribbean methodologies, Afrocentric trauma studies, and grassroots social transformation. It serves as a bridge between theory and practice, between pain and peace, and between who we ve been and who we are becoming.
Keywords: Afrocentric trauma healing, Caribbean co-parenting, intergenerational trauma, praxis, anti-fragility, post-traumatic growth, Sankofa, Jegnaship.
title: Sankofa Serenity: A Culturally Responsive Praxis Model for Trauma Healing in Caribbean Co-Parenting

description:
Over the years, police legitimacy has received extensive coverage because of its essential role in the criminal justice system and its importance in understanding criminals' decisionmaking processes. Despite this, not much research has been conducted on the topic within the Caribbean, thereby leaving gaps in its empirical understanding. Existing literature has however concluded that trust, fairness, compliance, respect, and effective procedural practices are the main building blocks of police legitimacy. These concepts are however intertwined into various types/forms of police legitimacy, resulting in the ongoing development with and around the topic. For this paper, theories such as procedural, liberal, social, independence, and, equity will be adopted to explain their (the new developments) rationales. These said theories will be further examined to assess the dichotomized views held by members of the public about the phenomenon and to understand the operation of police legitimacy within the Central Division of the Trinidad and Tobago Police Service. A qualitative research method, specifically, phenomenology will be used in analyzing data with interview sheets being the research tool.
Keywords: Police legitimacy, Policing, Residents' Perceptions, Central Division of the Trinidad and Tobago Police Service.
title: Understanding residents' perceptions of Police legitamacy within the Central Division of the Trinidad and Tobago Police Service

description: Political corruption severely undermines the political, economic, and social fabric of society. In Trinidad and Tobago (T&T), it remains a persistent and deeply entrenched challenge. Since the 1960s, political parties have consistently acknowledged the prevalence of corruption during election campaigns. They typically make two key promises: to address the widespread corruption if elected and to implement preventative measures alongside prosecuting those involved. Although some administrations have introduced anti-corruption policies once in office, T&T continues to carry the label of the 'land of bobol.'" Despite more than three decades of anti-corruption efforts
title: the most recent Corruption Perceptions Index from Transparency International indicates that T&T is still perceived as highly corrupt. Political corruption scandals are frequent
column5: there have been no successful prosecutions of politicians involved in such activities. This suggests that existing policies have neither effectively reduced corruption nor altered public perception. Many anticorruption scholars now agree that the anti-corruption policies of the last two decades have failed in many countries. There is a growing literature that explains why these antipolicies have failed. However
column6: there is a lack of empirical research investigating why anticorruption policies in T&T have failed. This study aims to fill that gap through a multiple case study approach
column7: focusing on two prominent political corruption scandals in T&T. Data will be gathered through document analysis of relevant laws and publicly available official reports
column8: as well as in-depth interviews with stakeholders from the public sector and civil society. Thematic analysis will be used to examine the data
column9: with particular attention to preventative measures
column11: and civil society engagement. The study is expected to yield critical insights into the shortcomings of current anti-corruption policies
column12: particularly those that relate to prevention
column15: insights from the study will be used to make recommendations for future research and for more effective strategies to combat political corruption in T&T and similar countries."
column16: Explaining the failure of anti-corruption policies in Trinidad and Tobago: A multiple case study approach

description: Criminal gangs and gang behaviour remain a prevalent and troubling phenomenon in several islands throughout the Caribbean. Trinidad and Tobago has especially experienced noteworthy gang membership throughout several communities. Much more alarming is the continuous and ongoing inter-generational attachment to gangs. Empirical research on the emergence of gangs has long been a desired and vibrant area of scholarship in the social sciences, particularly in the field of criminology and criminal justice. Historically, the body of literature in gangs has been broadly defined to incorporate a multitude of theoretical explanations for North American and European gangs but much of gang research has drastically expanded recently to include the global south and other continents across the world. Many of the earliest scholarships of gangs and gang membership have been highly criticized for their vagueness and inability to draw empirical conclusions on diverse populations. Nevertheless, past research has set the stage for multiple waves of studies on gangs and gang activity that have become much more methodologically and theoretically sophisticated. Consequently, knowledge on gangs has advanced to include critical information about gang formation as well as the additive effects of specific environmental factors, including family, socio-economic, health and subcultural dynamics. This current research study has focused on gangs in The Caribbean, with a relatively small sample of local gang members in Trinidad and Tobago. The expectation is that the research adds to scholarship on community and county level factors that might influence gang involvement. A significant aspect of this study is used to identify societal risk factors that possibly explain the appeal of gangs and then, provide prospective initiatives to reduce gang influence in the country. The purpose of this study is to assess environmental factors and its effects on gang membership in Trinidad and Tobago given the relatively high number of gangs that were established within the past five years. Furthermore, the research uses the social structure perspectives to provide a more comprehensive understanding of current gang affiliations in the country. The data used in the study were garnered from focus groups held with gang members in four communities in the Port-of-Spain Police Division in Trinidad and Tobago. Using a qualitative methodological approach, the findings suggest that community level factors contribute to the gang continuity. Furthermore, the gang culture in communities helps further explain the issue. The research demonstrates that community-specific initiatives are necessary for the reduction of criminal gang activities and gang behaviour.
title: Joining a Gang: An Assessment of Gang Affiliation in Crime-Prone Communities in Trinidad and Tobago

description:
Non-communicable diseases (NCDs) have emerged as a critical public health concern globally, and their disproportionate impact on marginalised populations is increasingly evident. This study addresses the knowledge and policy gaps regarding the prevalence and determinants of NCDs within Jamaica s lesbian, gay, bisexual and transgender (LGBT) population, an under-researched demographic often subjected to structural and social barriers that exacerbate health vulnerabilities. Conducted by the IDEA Research Centre, this research aims to provide empirical insights to support more inclusive health policies and interventions in Jamaica.
The research utilizes a mixed-methods approach, combining quantitative survey data with qualitative interviews to assess the incidence of key NCDs such as hypertension, diabetes, stroke, cardiovascular disease, various cancers, and various mental health conditions among LGBT individuals.
The findings highlight differences in NCD burden, revealing that the LGBT population in Jamaica experiences higher-than-average rates of certain NCDs when compared to national figures, with stress-related conditions, including anxiety and depression, being particularly prevalent. These health disparities are linked not only to behavioural risk factors but also to systemic challenges such as healthcare stigma and discrimination; limited access to culturally competent services; and socioeconomic exclusion. The survey also indicates higher prevalence of tobacco, marijuana and alcohol use among LGBT individuals, with specific patterns observed among lesbian and bisexual women.
The research identifies, among other things, stigma, minority stress, and experiences of discriminatory violence and harassment as critical psychosocial determinants contributing to the onset and progression of NCDs in this population. The findings highlight how these stressors, combined with fears of disclosure and previous negative encounters in medical settings, discourage LGBT individuals from seeking timely and adequate healthcare, ultimately leading to their inability to adopt preventive health behaviours.
The study concludes with recommendations aimed at addressing structural inequities. These include training healthcare providers in LGBT-affirming care, integrating sexual orientation and gender-identity data collection into national health surveys, expanding access to mental health services, integrating NCD-specific care into health services delivered to LGBT communities, and strengthening community-based outreach programmes. By highlighting the intersection of sexual orientation, gender identity, and non-communicable disease
vulnerability, this research underscores the urgent need for inclusive, rights-based public health strategies that leave no one behind.
This research contributes to the growing body of evidence advocating for the inclusion of LGBT communities in Jamaica s health development agenda, aligning with broader global commitments to health equity and the Sustainable Development Goals.
title: The Prevalence of NCDs in Jamaica's LBGT Population

description:
Brief introduction to the research/project: Routine health information systems (RHIS) collect data from multiple sources within the healthcare system and are used for the allocation of resources, policymaking, planning and patient management. Within the Lowand Middle-Income Countries (LMIC), there have been notable challenges related to the quality, timeliness, relevance and use of the RHIS data for research and policy development. As such, this paper utilises the Performance of Routine Information System Management (PRISM) framework as an evaluative tool to identify and outline existing barriers to the availability, quality and use of RHIS-data in Trinidad and Tobago.
Objective: To identify the barriers to quality, access and use of routine health information systems (RHIS) data in Trinidad and Tobago.
Methods: Through a descriptive qualitative approach, purposive sampling was used to recruit 19 users and producers of RHIS data to participate in key informant interviews. Interviews were conducted online via zoom, and pseudonyms were used to protect participants  identity. The de-identified and cleaned transcripts were then thematically analysed using Dedoose (Version 9.0.17). 
Results: The PRISM-based determinants highlighted the existing challenges to RHIS data quality, access and use in Trinidad and Tobago. The data demonstrated that there was a significant underutilization of RHIS data beyond patient management primarily due to data access and quality challenges. Bureaucratic processes, paper-based recording and storage systems, and ownership/security concerns around the data were most cited as major challenges to potential users. Additionally, participants described the lack of standardization of data collection tools and processes, training gaps for data collectors, data incompleteness, and technological and infrastructural constraints as present factors affecting the quality of TT-RHIS data. Combined, these issues posed significant challenges to the access and use of RHIS data for health research and policy development. Participants articulated a need for a national electronic health information system, adequate training of staff involved in data management, and a comprehensive monitoring and evaluation plan as possible solutions to the challenges around RHIS data quality, access, and use.
Conclusions: Strengthening the data governance and management of Trinidad and Tobago s RHIS could support more effective research and evidence-informed policymaking. Key to this is addressing issues around data quality and access with the expedited implementation of an interoperable national electronic data recording and storage system. Furthermore, this should be accompanied by investments to ensure adequate staffing and training, appropriate infrastructure and technological support.
title: Traversing Routine Health Information Systems in Trinidad and Tobago: A Qualitative Analysis

description:
Brief Introduction to the Research/Project: Sexually Transmitted Infections (STIs) remains a significant public health concern for healthcare systems, communities and individuals. There is limited literature in the Caribbean, specifically in Trinidad, examining the epidemiology of STIs among clinic attendees. Understanding the burden of disease and its risk factors associated in this population is important to develop effective prevention and treatment strategies.
Objective(s): To determine the prevalence and associated risk factors of STIs at two clinics in Trinidad.
Methods: A cross-sectional study of adults 18 years or older visiting the Queen s Park Counselling Centre and Clinic (QPCC&C) northern and southern clinics was conducted. Interviewer-administered questionnaires were collected from a convenience sample of 250 persons. Data on participants  socio-demographic characteristics, self-reported STI history during the previous 12 months, sexual orientation and behaviour and substance use were collected. STATA version 15 was used to generate descriptive statistics and conduct Chi-Squared test and logistic regression. Level of significance was set at 0.05.
Results: Of the 250 participants, the majority were under 30 years old (49%), female (53%), single (68%), Afro-Trinidadian (55%), had secondary level education or lower (68%), employed (55%), heterosexual (90%) and did not consume alcohol (39%). More than half (51%) used condoms sometimes and most did not use a condom during their last sexual encounter (76%). Self-reported STI prevalence was 28%, of which the most common STIs DBS Postgraduate Research Conference 2025 were gonorrhea (12%), herpes (6%) and syphilis (5%). There were significant sex-differences between STI prevalence and employment status, having multiple sexual partners and alcohol consumption (p < 0.05 each). Furthermore, socio-demographic characteristics and sexual orientation were associated with self-reported STI history: lower education level (OR: 6.14 (2.66   14.16), p < 0.001), low monthly household income (OR: 2.26 (1.08   4.71), p = 0.030), unemployed (OR: 2.81 (1.41   5.61), p < 0.001) and non-heterosexual persons (OR: 2.93 (1.28   6.69), p = 0.010).
Conclusion: These findings highlight the need for tailored interventions to prevent and control STIs, particularly in high-risk groups such as individuals with low education, low household income, unemployed people and members of sexual and gender minority groups.
title: Prevalence and Risk Factors Associated with Sexually Transmitted Infections in Trinidad and Tobago

description:
Introduction: Perspectives on mental illness are shaped by various factors, including cultural background, spiritual beliefs, personal experiences, community expectations, the media, and socioeconomic conditions (Subu et al., 2021). These perspectives are further influenced by socially constructed language that often carries negative connotations. Labels such as 'mad people,' 'crazy,' or 'mental' contribute to stigma and discrimination, impacting both individual understanding and community narratives about mental illness. Labels that carry negative connotations can be a significant obstacle to appropriate treatment for mental illnesses, impeding the achievement of optimal health outcomes. This study explores the perspectives on mental illness in a rural community in East Trinidad, where cultural beliefs and traditions, limited healthcare resources for mental health and wellness, and socioeconomic challenges play significant roles in shaping these narratives.
Objectives: The study aims to explore and understand the perspectives of rural community members on mental illness, with a focus on identifying how mental illness is viewed, what factors influence such attitudes, and recommendations to effect positive change.
Methodology: Using a qualitative approach, data were collected through semi-structured interviews with 15 participants at the local Community Centre. The semi-structured format encouraged participants to share their perspectives authentically, capturing nuanced insights into the lived experiences that shaped these perspectives on mental health and illness.
Results: Participants  definition of mental illness was people who had a brain dysfunction, people with erratic behaviours, and those who were irrational. Influencing factors included religious beliefs, media representations, cultural upbringing, socioeconomic challenges, and the lack of mental health awareness and services. Many participants stated that terms such as  mad people  and  Crazy people  were often used to describe people living with a mental health condition. They disclosed that they believed mental illness has a component of spirituality attached to it, and violence is also a primary characteristic of mental illness. They expressed dissatisfaction with the lack of mental health services in their area and believed that it was part of the reason people did not seek help. These findings underscore how stigmatizing labels and systemic barriers hinder mental health outcomes in underserved communities.
title: How do we View Mental Illness? Exploring the Perspectives of Persons on Mental Illness in a Rural Community in East Trinidad

description:
Background: Aging and retirement in the Caribbean, particularly in Trinidad and Tobago, are influenced by cultural, economic, and social dynamics. Key protective factors such as strong family ties, religious beliefs, and community involvement significantly support the psychological well-being of older adults (Bourne, McGrowder, & Crawford, 2015). However, retirees often face challenges like inadequate pension coverage, limited healthcare access, and the erosion of traditional family support systems due to migration and  industrialization (Maharaj & Reid, 2018). Despite these difficulties, many older persons find resilience through spiritual engagement and active participation in community groups (Bourne et al., 2015). In Jamaica and Barbados, similar cultural practices promote emotional strength and a sense of identity among seniors (George & Francis, 2018). Gender differences also exist, with older men facing greater difficulty adjusting to retirement, while women maintain stronger social ties through caregiving and religious roles (Maharaj & Reid, 2018). This study seeks to understand how retirees in Trinidad adapt to retirement, highlighting the need for research into the psychological processes that foster resilience and support mental health in later life.
Objectives: The primary objective of this study is to examine the resilience of older adults in Trinidad post-retirement, identifying the key factors that influence their ability to cope with the challenges associated with this transition. Specifically, the research aims to:
1. Identify individual and community-level factors (personal coping strategies, social support, and engagement in meaningful activities) that contribute to resilience among retirees in Trinidad.
2. Examine the psychological outcomes of resilience, including the impact on life satisfaction, mental health, and overall well-being among retired individuals.
3. Investigate the relationship between resilience and key stressors faced by retirees in Trinidad, such as health concerns, financial security, and social isolation.
4. Provide recommendations for improving resilience among retirees in Trinidad through community-based initiatives, social services, and government policies
Methodology: This study will use an explanatory sequential mixed methods design to explore resilience in retired adults in Trinidad. The quantitative phase will involve 200 retirees aged 60 and older, using standardized instruments such as the Resilience Scale for Older Adults (RSOA), Geriatric Depression Scale (GDS-15), Geriatric Anxiety Scale (GAS-10), Perceived Social Support Questionnaire (PSSQ-12), Social Isolation Scale (SIS-6), and a demographic survey. Participants will be recruited through community organizations, retirement homes, and online platforms. Those who consent will be invited for in-depth semistructured interviews, with 30 participants selected to provide qualitative insights into coping strategies and adjustment. Data will be collected via online and in-person methods following ethical approval. Quantitative data will be analyzed using SPSS-30 with statistical techniques including descriptive statistics, Pearson s correlation, t-tests, multiple and moderated regression, hierarchical regression, and mediation analyses to test the hypotheses related to resilience, well-being, and social factors. Qualitative data will undergo thematic analysis using QDA Miner to identify coping themes. The combined approach will provide a comprehensive understanding of the psychological and social mechanisms supporting resilience in retired adults in Trinidad.
title: Resilience in Retirement: Exploring the Factors Influencing Resilience In Older Adults

description:
Introduction: Occupational therapy is recommended as a key health discipline in service delivery for people living with dementia. However, people from the Black Caribbean, Black African and Black British community have a 20% higher incidence of dementia compared to the UK average. People from Black Caribbean communities present later to dementia services, they present to services with more advance disease, and are less likely to receive a dementia diagnosis.There is limited understanding of the resistance to engage with the formal health system on the part of people living with dementia from Black, African, Caribbean backgrounds. These conditions are strikingly similar, although for different reasons, to what currently obtains in the Caribbean. Due to the rapidly ageing population,the number of people aged 60 and older living with dementia in Latin America and the Caribbean was estimated at 6.86 million in 2020, projected to increase to 19.33 million by 2050. In light of these predictions, and in the absence of a cure, it is critical that health policy makers identify and apply effective solutions to this growing social health problem. Psychosocial interventions employing successful mental health models such as the CHIME (Connectedness Hope and Optimism for the future, Identity, Meaning in life, Empowerment) Framework have shown promise for implementation and cultural translation to Caribbean contexts using an emerging conceptual model.
Objective(s):
1. To systematically appraise the CHIME Framework in the context of dementia care.
2. To explore the suitability of the CHIME Framework from the experience from the perspective of ethnically diverse UK practicing occupational therapists.
3. To generate plausible implementation strategies for innovative dementia care service delivery in Caribbean contexts.
Methodology: This research followed the Medical Research Council (MRC) Framework on complex interventions and focused on the development stage of theory building. A systematic review was conducted, followed by an exploratory descriptive qualitative study, with a focus on the Black Caribbean British experience, which was conducted with ethnically diverse occupational therapists around the suitability of the CHIME approach for people living with mild dementia. The findings from both studies were synthesised and theoretically mapped against the core elements of the MRC to produce an explanatory model which was then developed into programme theories.
Results: Thematic analysis of the synthesised data conceptualised five main themes around recovery-oriented occupational therapy dementia practice: 1. Flexible modes of technology, 2. Adaptive interventions, 3. Culturally humble practitioner approach 4. Explicit application of CHIME and 5. Transformative learning. The findings suggest that a CHIMEinformed, culturally sensitive approach in occupational therapy practice could enhance the therapeutic encounter and could improve engagement for people affected by mild dementia particularly from minoritised ethnic groups. Initial programme theory development generated contextually??relevant, implementable strategies based on plausible mechanisms of change. These findings and methodologies are significant in that they combine the transdisciplinary areas of health equity research and clinical practice engendering a praxis for social change of a complex social health problem.
title: Translating the CHIME Framework in Occupational Therapy to Bring Hope to Caribbean Dementia Care: Lessons from the Black Caribbean British Dementia Experience

description:
Introduction: Currently, there is an extensive area of scholarship around the topic of body image in academia. Predominantly examined within the discipline of psychology, existing research highlights the significant connections between body image issues and wellbeing, particularly among youth populations. Identified as an area of global health concern, body image has been connected to eating disorders, non-suicidal self-injury, and other unhealthy behaviours and practices. Interrogations into these have led to a conceptual expansion of body image, to include areas such as body functionality and body image experiences. This expansion has signalled a call for more social investigations into the construction and experience of body image, with considerations to factors such as socioeconomic class, race, and in particular, gender. While studies have been conducted within the Caribbean, there is a lack of social engagement with body image and body image experiences, that recognises the presence and role of social factors in constructing and shaping how young populations experience gendered body image. Thus, this paper is an initial foray into body image and body image experiences through the lens of the socioecological framework and qualitative methodology.
Objective(s): To explore the social construction of young women s body image and body image experiences in Trinidad.
